Patch Set 3:

Found an important bug in the test - When we don't append to the file, we truncate it and the set {u,g}id bit is cleared during that operation.  Fixed now.

Note that this should have the effect of reversing which test failed on a newer kernel, and that the is_sxid check used by older kernels is in fact correct.  Oops.  (The true IS_NOSEC still doesn't work, though.)

Patch Set 4:

This one's a bit of a mess.  I'd appreciate careful review.

I am pretty confident I am only setting S_NOSEC when it can safely and correctly be set, but I am concerned that we should be trying harder to keep it set at all relevant times.  The current patch is safe, but not might be optimal.

Note that with 8 parallel writers to one file I saw 1 case in 70,000 where a writer took the i_mutex lock unnecessarily.  So there's basically zero possibility of waiting because of that.  (A more complex workload might cause this more often, but those should be rare.)

Patch Set 4: Code-Review-1

This needs a tweak...  in ll_update_inode, we need to remove the S_NOSEC flag (IE, clear i_flags) before we change the permissions.  So just move the order of some things around in there.

Patch Set 4:

Here's the larger, messier update.

I no longer think XFS - or anyone, though it looks like no one else tries [XFS is the only filesystem which checks S_NOSEC] - handles this correctly.

XFS checks IS_NOSEC twice.  Once to decide if it should take the i_mutex, then again to decide if it should actually call file_remove_privs.  But the flag could be changed in the interim.  If it is cleared after we read it the first time (choosing not to take the i_mutex), then we will do the file_remove_privs operation without holding the i_mutex.  (This is the code which insists we hold it.)

It's possible there's some piece of XFS specific locking I've missed that protects the i_flags from being changed, but it doesn't look like it.  S_NOSEC is cleared in the generic setxattr code of the kernel.

We can't have the two separate checks of IS_NOSEC/S_NOSEC.  We need just one, unless we hold the i_mutex for the second one.  IE, we need to take the i_mutex and call file_remove_privs on the basis of the same check of IS_NOSEC.  (There is still a race where the permissions can be modified during the write [setxattr takes the i_mutex], but that doesn't seem to be something XFS is trying to handle, so we'll ignore it.)

That means we need a new version of __generic_file_write_iter which does not expect the i_mutex to be held (as opposed to just not taking it).  I think the only change is to push file_remove_privs out of there in to the caller.

Andreas,

Thoughts?  I'm feeling like I should possibly take this to the LKML.  Certainly it looks to be wrong upstream.

Patch Set 4:

Hm, OK.  I don't have a stack trace handy, but here's the relevant paths.

In SLES 12 and CentOS 6, "file_remove_privs" doesn't exist.  The equivalent is file_remove_suid, which is called from __generic_file_aio_write.

In the current kernel from Linus, file_remove_privs is called from a number of filesystems.  The call that's relevant for Lustre is from __generic_file_write_iter.

Patch Set 4:

"since if i_mutex is not held at the time of the first check it means SUID is not set, and file_remove_privs() does not need to be called in this case to clear it."
Right, the problem is if SUID gets set after that point, we will call notify_change without the i_mutex held, because the choice to call file_remove_privs is made separately from the choice to take or not take the i_mutex.

Patch Set 4:

Sure, but enabling SUID is not in itself a security risk, unlike keeping it set when the file is rewritten. At worst this will cause a WARN_ON_ONCE() in a very rare code path, which AFAIK is otherwise totally harmless. I think the main concern for the locking is that SUID is cleared if the file is rewritten. 

You might be able to trigger it with two loops, one doing "chmod u+S" and the other rewriting the file, but I suspect you can do the same with XFS, and in the end there is no harm done.

Patch Set 4: Code-Review-1

(8 comments)

Patch Set 5: Code-Review-1

(1 comment)

We're also chasing what may be a self-deadlock on CentOS 7 with this patch.  Seen at Cray in sanity testing - test 233b.

Patch Set 5:

Here's the stack trace of the deadlock:
 #0 [ffff88003574f860] __schedule at ffffffff8163a41d
 #1 [ffff88003574f8c8] schedule_preempt_disabled at ffffffff8163bb99
 #2 [ffff88003574f8d8] __mutex_lock_slowpath at ffffffff81639895
 #3 [ffff88003574f930] mutex_lock at ffffffff81638cff
 #4 [ffff88003574f948] ll_iget at ffffffffa0dd0df8 [lustre]
 #5 [ffff88003574f998] ll_prep_inode at ffffffffa0dc1e60 [lustre]
 #6 [ffff88003574fa58] ll_lookup_it_finish at ffffffffa0dd16c7 [lustre]
 #7 [ffff88003574fb10] ll_lookup_it at ffffffffa0dd272d [lustre]
 #8 [ffff88003574fbc8] ll_lookup_nd at ffffffffa0dd443e [lustre]
 #9 [ffff88003574fc60] lookup_real at ffffffff811e85ed
#10 [ffff88003574fc80] __lookup_hash at ffffffff811e8f62
#11 [ffff88003574fcb0] lookup_slow at ffffffff816326cb
#12 [ffff88003574fce8] path_lookupat at ffffffff811ede33
#13 [ffff88003574fd80] filename_lookup at ffffffff811ede8b
#14 [ffff88003574fdb8] user_path_at_empty at ffffffff811efc57
#15 [ffff88003574fe88] user_path_at at ffffffff811efcc1
#16 [ffff88003574fe98] vfs_fstatat at ffffffff811e3503
#17 [ffff88003574fee8] SYSC_newlstat at ffffffff811e3ad1
#18 [ffff88003574ff70] sys_newlstat at ffffffff811e3d5e
#19 [ffff88003574ff80] system_call_fastpath at ffffffff81645b09

As noted, it's a self deadlock.

I think the mutex is being taken in lookup_slow, which takes the i_mutex of the parent.

Here's the test:
test233b:
        local fid=$($LFS path2fid $MOUNT/.lustre)
        stat $MOUNT/.lustre/fid/$fid > /dev/null ||
                error "cannot access $MOUNT/.lustre using its FID '$fid'"

I suspect there's a loop here and we're our own parent.  I wonder how XFS avoids this problem...

Is that sort of loop possible in 'normal' usage?  Or is it just a function of this magic directory in Lustre?

Hm.

Patch Set 5:

Ah.  Hard links are banned for directories, presumably so we can avoid situations like this.  (And there's no reason symbolic links should cause this.)

The specific problematic call is this pair:
fid=$($LFS path2fid $MOUNT/.lustre/fid)
stat $MOUNT/.lustre/fid/$fid

Because in this situation, 'fid' is literally its own parent.

Log:
lmv_intent_lock()) INTENT LOCK 'lookup' for [0x2fd80001:0x284abf35:0x0] '' on [0x2fd80001:0x284abf35:0x0]

Have to think about how to fix this.  Presumably lookup of any fid under 'fid' should work, but...  Yuck.

Patch Set 5:

Andreas - Would it be possible to remove the offending part of the test?

We can still test open-by-fid of ".lustre", just not of the "fid" subdirectory.  I can't imagine anyone actually wanting to do open-by-fid of "fid".

Patch Set 5: Code-Review-1

Since this would result in a deadlock that a user could trigger easily, probably what is needed is to reject (or special case without any lookup) the ".lustre/fid" FID early in the callpath.

Patch Set 5:

I've settled (tentatively) on rejecting it and on how/where to do it.

What error would you prefer?  lfs fid2path gives '-ENODATA' when called with the fid of ./lustre/fid, so perhaps that?  I'll put that in my patch unless I hear from you first (which I'll upload once I've sorted out a build problem).
Uploaded patch set 6.

This version is just getting testing at Cray, I'm uploading it partly to get autotest results.

Patch Set 10:

I'm not aware of any, even after a quick look around. The reason this is safe here is because the extent tree locking replaces the i_mutex usage, and I don't know that anywhere else in Lustre shares that characteristic of having a different mechanism for thread safety.  So it seems like that would be out of bounds in general.

But maybe you're thinking of something that hasn't occurred to me...?

(also, could you restart testing?  Thank you!)

parents.subject: LU-9019 libcfs: remove no longer use time abstraction

commit.subject: LU-8656 vvp: Add S_NOSEC support
commit.message:
LU-8656 vvp: Add S_NOSEC support

We must use the i_mutex to protect permission changes,
which means we need to take it when we write to a file with
the setuid or setgid bit set (as this removes those bits).

LU-8025 attempted to use IS_NOSEC to check for this case,
but did not actually add support for the S_NOSEC flag to
Lustre.

S_NOSEC was added in upstream kernel commit:
69b4573296469fd3f70cf7044693074980517067
But a key change requiring parallel filesystems to opt in
with a superblock flag was added in:
9e1f1de02c2275d7172e18dc4e7c2065777611bf

This patch adds the required support.

Specifically, Lustre should set S_NOSEC correctly when an
inode is created (ll_iget), but only for new inodes.
Setting it for existing inodes requires taking the i_mutex,
creating an unacceptable metadata performance impact in the
lookup code.

Existing inodes get S_NOSEC set either by a setattr call
(see below), or by the first writer to write to the node,
in file_remove_privs/file_remove_suid.  So it's OK not to
set S_NOSEC on all inodes in ll_iget.

Also, Lustre must clear S_NOSEC when it gets a metadata
update because another node could have changed permissions.
i_flags is already cleared in ll_update_inode, but we would
prefer to have S_NOSEC set whenever possible, so we want to
re-do the check after the update.

This requires holding the i_mutex to avoid a check/set race
with permissions changes.

We cannot easily take the i_mutex in ll_update_inode (it is
called from too many places, some of which already hold the
i_mutex).

It is acceptable to sometimes not have S_NOSEC set (since
occasionally taking the i_mutex when not needed is OK), so
we look at getting it set most of the time.

It looks like the primary concern is ll_md_setattr.  The
caller (ll_setattr_raw) takes the i_mutex before returning,
so we do the relevant call there.

This opens a window during which S_NOSEC is not set, but
again, this merely creates a situation where we take the
i_mutex unnecessarily, and should be rare in practice.

Testing with multiple writers shows that we only very
rarely attempt to take the i_mutex, so the performance
impact is minimal.

This commit also adds a test to verify the S_NOSEC/i_mutex
behavior.

Signed-off-by: Patrick Farrell <paf@cray.com>
Change-Id: If481366283ceaeb247e50cc4d536fb10e9c329ee
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/22853
Tested-by: Jenkins
Reviewed-by: James Simmons <uja.ornl@yahoo.com>
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <andreas.dilger@intel.com>
